title: Hummus
subtitle: Smooth, tahini-forward chickpea dip from the Levantine tradition.
excerpt: Proper hummus takes twenty minutes and two tins of chickpeas. The method matters: blend the tahini and lemon before the chickpeas go in, add ice water to loosen, and keep blending long past the point where it looks done. The result is pale, silky, and nothing like the tub from the supermarket.
sourceNotes: The dish appears in medieval Arabic manuscripts and across Ottoman-era cookery texts. The tahini-and-chickpea combination is documented in thirteenth-century Syrian cookbooks (Manuscrit arabe 7009, Bibliothèque nationale de France). The ratios and technique here follow the traditional Levantine domestic method, cross-referenced with established proportions from the Palestinian and Lebanese home-cooking canon.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. A food processor handles up to four tins without straining. Keep the finished hummus in the fridge for up to three days, pressed with cling film directly against the surface to prevent drying.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to three days ahead. Bring to room temperature for thirty minutes before serving and stir in a tablespoon of cold water to restore the texture if it has thickened.

glossaryTerms.definition: A paste made from ground sesame seeds, used as a base fat and flavouring in Levantine and North African cooking. Good tahini is pale, fluid, and faintly bitter; thick or dark tahini will give a chalky, roasted flavour. Stir the jar before measuring — the oil and solids separate on standing.

content.text: The simplest hummus is made in the food processor with two tins of chickpeas, good

, lemon, and a little ice water. The version that takes a day (soaking dried chickpeas, cooking them soft, peeling each one individually) is better, but this one is very good and you can make it tonight. The difference between good home hummus and the shop-bought tub is almost entirely in the technique, not the ingredients.

Two things matter most. First, blend the tahini and lemon juice together before the chickpeas go in. The acid makes the tahini seize and turn pale and thick, which is what you want; it lightens the colour and the flavour. Second, add ice water a tablespoon at a time while the machine runs. The cold water aerates the hummus as it blends, which is what gives it that pale, almost mousse-like texture when it is done properly. Keep blending long past the point where it looks finished; two full minutes in a decent food processor is the minimum for a properly smooth result.

The finish is a matter of taste. Some cooks stop at smooth; others blend until the hummus is almost white. A good drizzle of olive oil and a pinch of cumin or paprika on top is the traditional presentation. Whole chickpeas scattered across the surface add texture and make it look as though you thought about it.

items.prepNote: two 400 g tins, drained and rinsed

content.text: A food processor is the right tool; a stick blender will work but delivers a coarser texture. You will also need a cup of ice water to add while blending.

Put {{tahini}} of tahini, the juice of {{lemon}} lemon, and {{garlic}} of crushed garlic in the food processor. Run for one full minute. The mixture will seize and turn thick and pale — this is correct. Scrape down the sides.

Add {{chickpeas-tinned}} of chickpeas to the food processor along with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil and a generous pinch of salt. Run for thirty seconds, then, with the machine still running, add ice water a tablespoon at a time through the feed tube. Keep adding and blending for at least two full minutes; three if you want a very silky result. The hummus should be pale, smooth, and hold a soft swirl when you run a spoon through it.

Taste and adjust. More lemon for brightness; more tahini for nuttiness; more salt if the flavour is flat. Spread onto a wide, shallow plate, drawing a spiral with the back of a spoon to create a hollow. Drizzle with olive oil, dust with a pinch of ground cumin or sweet paprika if you like, and scatter whole chickpeas on top.

items.symptom: The hummus is thick and stiff
items.cause: Not enough water added during blending, or the tahini was dry.
items.fix: Add ice water a tablespoon at a time and blend for another minute. The texture loosens noticeably with each addition.

items.cause: Old or dark tahini, or too much garlic.
items.fix: Reduce the garlic to half a clove next time. Check the tahini — it should smell faintly nutty, not rancid.

items.symptom: The flavour is flat despite seasoning
items.fix: Add lemon juice by the teaspoon, blending between each addition, until the flavour sharpens.
Scrape down the bowl and run the food processor for a further two minutes, adding a tablespoon of ice water halfway through.

content.text: Hummus bi tahini appears in medieval Arabic texts as early as the thirteenth century, long before it became the universal mezze plate of modern Levantine cookery. The dish anchors the mezze spread from Beirut to Amman, served at every meal from breakfast (with a fried egg and olive oil pooled in the hollow) to dinner as a side dish beside grilled meats and flatbread. Its presence in UK supermarkets since the 1990s has made it one of the most widely eaten Middle Eastern foods in Britain; the domestic version rewards the slight extra effort of making it yourself.
